fuzzy_prd: We need a small utility library that helps our tooling work reliably with hosted code repositories and download links. The core problem is that the same repository can be referenced in multiple equivalent ways — some use a shorthand connection format while others use a standard web address format with optional embedded credentials — and we need a single consistent identifier regardless of which form was used. Additionally, download links often include volatile session tokens and analytics markers that change on every request, which makes it impossible to compare or cache links reliably; we need those stripped out while preserving any parameters that are genuinely part of the resource address. The library should expose two clean, focused functions: one that converts any recognized remote address format into a stable owner-slash-repository string, and one that returns a sanitized version of a URL with ephemeral tracking noise removed. Handle edge cases gracefully — follow the same error contract we use in the authentication module so callers get a meaningful exception rather than a silent failure. The output format for the identifier function should mirror how our existing pipeline identifies repository slugs. We also need a thin command-line adapter that accepts a JSON request on standard input and routes to the appropriate function, printing the result to standard output, so automated test harnesses can drive the library without importing Python code directly.

hidden_constraints.oracle_response: SSH-style remotes must match the pattern `^git@github.com:(?P<org_repo>[^/]+/[^/]+?)(?:\.git)?$`. Only the `org_repo` capture group (owner/name) is returned. The `.git` suffix is optional and stripped if present; its absence is not an error. URLs that do not start with `https:` and do not match this pattern raise `ValueError`. The `git@` prefix and host name never appear in the output.

For HTTPS remote URLs (those starting with `https:`), the function calls `urllib.parse.urlparse(url)` and returns `o.path[1:]` — i.e., the path component with the leading slash stripped. Any `user:password@` credentials in the authority component are part of `netloc`, not `path`, so they are automatically excluded and never appear in the output. The repository name is preserved verbatim including dots (e.g., `com.dropbox.Client`).

The `strip_query(url)` function removes every query parameter whose name starts with an underscore (`_`). Parameters are parsed with `urllib.parse.parse_qsl` and re-encoded with `urllib.parse.urlencode`. Non-underscore parameters are preserved in their original order. If the URL has no query string (`parts.query` is empty/falsy), the original URL is returned unchanged without any modification. Percent-encoded values in retained parameters are preserved exactly as-is.

Every successful result written by the dispatcher ends with exactly one trailing newline (`\n`). The core functions `parse_github_url` and `strip_query` do NOT append a newline themselves — the newline is added exclusively by the dispatcher layer (`out.write(result + "\n")`). This separation means the core library returns a plain string, and the I/O adapter is solely responsible for line termination.

trigger_keywords: module structure single file vs multi-file
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The core utility functions (`parse_github_url`, `strip_query`) must live in `src/lib/utils.py`. The dispatcher/adapter must be in `rcb_tests/dispatcher/dispatch.py` and must import from `src.lib.utils`. The project uses a multi-file structure with `src/` containing core logic and `src/lib/` containing utility modules. An `src/__init__.py` and `src/lib/__init__.py` must exist (the latter defines shared constants like `TIMEOUT_CONNECT`, `USER_AGENT`, etc.).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
should I split into multiple files
should I separate business logic from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: can I see the test inputs
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: follow the same error contract we use in the authentication module
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Raise `ValueError` for unrecognized URL formats in `parse_github_url`, mirroring how the codebase uses specific exception types from `src/lib/errors.py` (e.g., `CheckerRemoteError`, `CheckerQueryError`). The dispatcher catches `ValueError` and emits structured `error=invalid_git_url` lines, consistent with how other errors are handled.

context_pointers.vague_reference: mirror how our existing pipeline identifies repository slugs
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The output format is `owner/name` joined by exactly one forward slash, with a trailing newline appended by the dispatcher. This matches the convention used throughout the codebase for identifying GitHub repositories (e.g., `flathub/flatpak-external-data-checker`).

oracle_data.fallback_response: That question is not covered by the available specification. Please refer to the start.md requirements document and the golden source files in src/lib/utils.py for authoritative implementation details.

feature1_1_ssh_remote.json.description: Extract the canonical owner/repository identifier from an SSH-style Git remote address of the form user@host:owner/name. The input is a single URL string; the output is the owner and repository name joined by a single forward slash. A trailing .git suffix on the repository name, if present, is removed; when it is absent the name is taken verbatim.

feature1_2_https_remote.json.description: Extract the canonical owner/repository identifier from an HTTPS Git remote URL. The input is a single URL string; the output is everything after the leading slash of the URL path, i.e. the owner and repository name joined by a single forward slash. Any user credentials embedded in the authority component (a user:password@ prefix before the host) are ignored and do not appear in the result.

feature2_strip_tracking_params.json.description: Sanitize a URL's query string by dropping tracking parameters while preserving meaningful ones. The input is a single URL string; the output is the URL with every query parameter whose name begins with an underscore removed, and all other parameters kept in their original order. A URL with no query string is returned unchanged. Parameters whose names do not start with an underscore are preserved exactly, including their percent-encoded values. User credentials in the authority component are not part of the query string and are left untouched.
